title: Sneewittchen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 53. Text from Wikisource („Sneewittchen (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The fifty-third tale (KHM 53), Snow White, under its 1857 title Sneewittchen. A queen’s envy of her stepdaughter’s beauty drives the mirror’s verdict, the huntsman’s mercy, the seven dwarfs, and three murder attempts ending in the poisoned apple — told without softening, down to the red-hot iron shoes. Older forms such as roth, todt, gieng and Athem are kept.

sentences.t: Es war einmal mitten im Winter, und die Schneeflocken fielen wie Federn vom Himmel herab, da saß eine Königin an einem Fenster, das einen Rahmen von schwarzem Ebenholz hatte, und nähte.
sentences.l: It was once in the midst of winter, and the snowflakes fell like feathers down from the sky, then sat a queen at a window that had a frame of black ebony, and sewed.
sentences.i: Once, in the depth of winter, when the snowflakes were falling from the sky like feathers, a queen sat sewing at a window that had a frame of black ebony.
sentences.n: The classic opening “Es war einmal” = “Once upon a time.” Note the framing verb pushed to the end (“und nähte,” “and sewed”); German sentences often close on the verb. Ebenholz (ebony) sets up the third of the three colors.

sentences.t: Und wie sie so nähte und nach dem Schnee aufblickte, stach sie sich mit der Nadel in den Finger, und es fielen drei Tropfen Blut in den Schnee.
sentences.l: And as she so sewed and looked up toward the snow, she pricked herself with the needle in the finger, and there fell three drops of blood into the snow.
sentences.i: And as she sewed, looking up at the snow, she pricked her finger with the needle, and three drops of blood fell into the snow.
sentences.n: “stach sie sich … in den Finger”: German says she pricked herself in the finger (dative of the person + the body part), where English says “pricked her finger.” aufblicken (to look up) is separable — the prefix here stays fused in the subordinate clause.

sentences.t: Und weil das Rothe im weißen Schnee so schön aussah, dachte sie bei sich „hätt ich ein Kind so weiß wie Schnee, so roth wie Blut, und so schwarz wie das Holz an dem Rahmen.“
sentences.l: And because the red in the white snow looked so beautiful, she thought to herself “had I a child as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black as the wood on the frame.”
sentences.i: And because the red against the white snow looked so lovely, she thought to herself: “If only I had a child as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black as the wood of the frame.”
sentences.n: “hätt ich” (= hätte ich) is subjunctive expressing a wish — “if only I had.” The three colors named here — white, red, black — are the wish that shapes the whole tale.

sentences.t: Bald darauf bekam sie ein Töchterlein, das war so weiß wie Schnee, so roth wie Blut, und so schwarzhaarig wie Ebenholz, und ward darum das Sneewittchen (Schneeweißchen) genannt.
sentences.l: Soon thereafter she got a little-daughter, that was as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black-haired as ebony, and was therefore called Sneewittchen (Little-Snow-White).
sentences.i: Soon afterward she had a little daughter, who was as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black-haired as ebony, and so she was named Sneewittchen (Little Snow-White).
sentences.n: Töchterlein is a diminutive of Tochter (daughter). Sneewittchen is a Low-German form; the text glosses it with High German Schneeweißchen. ward is the old past of werden (became/was) — you will meet it often here.

sentences.t: Und wie das Kind geboren war, starb die Königin.
sentences.l: And as the child was born, the queen died.
sentences.i: And when the child was born, the queen died.
sentences.n: wie here means “when/as,” not “how.” starb is the past of sterben (to die).

sentences.t: Über ein Jahr nahm sich der König eine andere Gemahlin.
sentences.l: After a year the king took himself another consort.
sentences.i: After a year the king took another wife.
sentences.n: “Über ein Jahr” = after a year’s time. “nahm sich … eine Gemahlin”: the reflexive sich (to himself) is idiomatic; Gemahlin is an elevated word for wife/consort.

sentences.t: Es war eine schöne Frau, aber sie war stolz und übermüthig, und konnte nicht leiden daß sie an Schönheit von jemand sollte übertroffen werden.
sentences.l: She was a beautiful woman, but she was proud and overweening, and could not bear that she should be surpassed in beauty by anyone.
sentences.i: She was a beautiful woman, but proud and haughty, and could not bear that anyone should surpass her in beauty.
sentences.n: “nicht leiden können” = to be unable to stand/bear. übermüthig (1857 spelling of übermütig) = haughty, overweening. The passive “von jemand … übertroffen werden” = to be surpassed by someone.

sentences.t: Sie hatte einen wunderbaren Spiegel, wenn sie vor den trat und sich darin beschaute, sprach sie
sentences.l: She had a wondrous mirror; when she stepped before it and beheld herself therein, she spoke
sentences.i: She had a wondrous mirror, and when she stepped before it and looked at herself in it, she said:
sentences.n: “vor den trat”: den points back to the mirror (“before it”). sich beschauen = to look at oneself. The sentence leads straight into the famous rhyme.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”
sentences.i: “Mirror, mirror on the wall, / who is the fairest of us all?”
sentences.n: The tale’s refrain. Spieglein is a diminutive of Spiegel (mirror); the -ein ending and the rhyme Wand / Land carry the incantation. “die schönste” = the most beautiful (fem.). You will hear this seven times — a marker of the queen’s obsession.

sentences.t: so antwortete der Spiegel
sentences.l: so answered the mirror
sentences.i: the mirror answered:

sentences.t: „Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste im Land.“
sentences.l: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest in the land.”
sentences.n: “ihr seid”: the mirror addresses the queen with the old respectful plural ihr (“you are”), like French vous. Frau Königin = “Lady Queen,” a courtly address.

sentences.t: Da war sie zufrieden, denn sie wußte daß der Spiegel die Wahrheit sagte.
sentences.l: Then she was content, for she knew that the mirror told the truth.
sentences.i: Then she was satisfied, for she knew the mirror spoke the truth.
sentences.n: wußte (1857 spelling of wusste) is the past of wissen (to know). daß (= dass) opens the “that”-clause and sends its verb to the end.

sentences.t: Sneewittchen aber wuchs heran, und wurde immer schöner, und als es sieben Jahr alt war, war es so schön, wie der klare Tag, und schöner als die Königin selbst.
sentences.l: But Sneewittchen grew up, and became ever more beautiful, and when it was seven years old, it was as beautiful as the clear day, and more beautiful than the queen herself.
sentences.i: But Snow-White grew up, and became ever lovelier, and when she was seven years old she was as fair as the bright day, and fairer than the queen herself.
sentences.n: German Sneewittchen is grammatically neuter (das Kind, the -chen diminutive), so the pronouns are es (it, nom./acc.) and ihm (to it, dat.), with the possessive sein — a girl is “it” in the grammar. English switches to “she.” “sieben Jahr” keeps the old uninflected Jahr after a number.

sentences.t: Als diese einmal ihren Spiegel fragte
sentences.l: When this one once asked her mirror
sentences.i: When she once asked her mirror:
sentences.n: diese (“this one”) points back to the queen. fragte = asked.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: so antwortete er
sentences.l: so answered he
sentences.i: he answered:
sentences.n: The mirror is grammatically masculine (der Spiegel), so er = “he.”

sentences.t:
„Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste hier,
aber Sneewittchen ist tausendmal schöner als ihr.“
sentences.l:
“Lady Queen, you are the fairest here,
but Sneewittchen is a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.i: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest here, / but Snow-White is a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.n: The turn: the mirror can no longer name the queen the fairest. tausendmal = a thousand times.

sentences.t: Da erschrack die Königin, und ward gelb und grün vor Neid.
sentences.l: Then the queen was startled, and turned yellow and green with envy.
sentences.i: Then the queen was aghast, and turned yellow and green with envy.
sentences.n: erschrack (1857 form of erschrak) is the past of erschrecken (to be startled, take fright). “gelb und grün vor Neid” — to go “yellow and green with envy” is the German idiom (we say “green with envy”).

sentences.t: Von Stund an, wenn sie Sneewittchen erblickte, kehrte sich ihr das Herz im Leibe herum, so haßte sie das Mädchen.
sentences.l: From that hour on, whenever she caught sight of Sneewittchen, her heart turned over within her body, so did she hate the girl.
sentences.i: From that hour on, whenever she caught sight of Snow-White, her heart turned over inside her, so did she hate the girl.
sentences.n: “Von Stund an” = from that hour on (Stund is the short old form of Stunde). erblickte = caught sight of. “kehrte sich … herum”: herumkehren (to turn round) is separable.

sentences.t: Und der Neid und Hochmuth wuchsen wie ein Unkraut in ihrem Herzen immer höher, daß sie Tag und Nacht keine Ruhe mehr hatte.
sentences.l: And the envy and haughtiness grew like a weed in her heart ever higher, so that she had no more rest, day and night.
sentences.i: And envy and pride grew ever higher like a weed in her heart, so that she had no peace day or night.
sentences.n: Hochmuth (1857 for Hochmut) = arrogance. Unkraut = weed. höher is the comparative of hoch (high).

sentences.t: Da rief sie einen Jäger und sprach „bring das Kind hinaus in den Wald, ich wills nicht mehr vor meinen Augen sehen.
sentences.l: Then she called a huntsman and spoke “bring the child out into the forest, I will no longer see it before my eyes.
sentences.i: Then she summoned a huntsman and said: “Take the child out into the forest; I will not have it before my eyes any longer.
sentences.n: “ich wills nicht … sehen”: wills = will es (want it), contracted. The queen’s command begins here and runs into the next unit.

sentences.t: Du sollst es tödten, und mir Lunge und Leber zum Wahrzeichen mitbringen.“
sentences.l: You shall kill it, and bring me lung and liver as a token.”
sentences.i: You shall kill it, and bring me its lungs and liver as proof.”
sentences.n: tödten (1857 for töten) = to kill. Wahrzeichen = a token or proof. The queen wants the organs as evidence the deed is done — a folk-tale motif.

sentences.t: Der Jäger gehorchte und führte es hinaus, und als er den Hirschfänger gezogen hatte und Sneewittchens unschuldiges Herz durchbohren wollte, fieng es an zu weinen und sprach „ach, lieber Jäger, laß mir mein Leben; ich will in den wilden Wald laufen und nimmermehr wieder heim kommen.“
sentences.l: The huntsman obeyed and led it out, and as he had drawn the hunting-knife and wanted to pierce Sneewittchen’s innocent heart, it began to weep and spoke “ah, dear huntsman, leave me my life; I will run into the wild forest and nevermore come home again.”
sentences.i: The huntsman obeyed and led her out, and just as he had drawn his hunting-knife and was about to pierce Snow-White’s innocent heart, she began to weep and said: “Ah, dear huntsman, spare my life; I will run off into the wild forest and never come home again.”
sentences.n: Hirschfänger = a hunting-knife (literally “deer-catcher”). fieng (1857 for fing) is the past of anfangen — “fieng … an,” separable, “began.” “laß mir mein Leben” = leave/spare me my life.

sentences.t: Und weil es so schön war, hatte der Jäger Mitleiden und sprach „so lauf hin, du armes Kind.“
sentences.l: And because it was so beautiful, the huntsman had pity and spoke “so run away, you poor child.”
sentences.i: And because she was so beautiful, the huntsman took pity and said: “Run off, then, you poor child.”
sentences.n: “Mitleiden haben” = to have pity, take pity. “so lauf hin”: hinlaufen (to run off) is separable.

sentences.t: „Die wilden Thiere werden dich bald gefressen haben“ dachte er, und doch wars ihm als wär ein Stein von seinem Herzen gewälzt, weil er es nicht zu tödten brauchte.
sentences.l: “The wild beasts will soon have devoured you” thought he, and yet it was to him as if a stone had been rolled from his heart, because he did not need to kill it.
sentences.i: “The wild beasts will soon devour you,” he thought, and yet it was as if a stone had been rolled from his heart, because he did not have to kill her.
sentences.n: Thiere (1857 for Tiere) = beasts. “ein Stein vom Herzen gewälzt” — a stone rolled from one’s heart is the German idiom for great relief. wär (= wäre) and “gewälzt” form a subjunctive “as if.”

sentences.t: Und als gerade ein junger Frischling daher gesprungen kam, stach er ihn ab, nahm Lunge und Leber heraus, und brachte sie als Wahrzeichen der Königin mit.
sentences.l: And as just then a young wild-boar came springing along, he stabbed it, took out lung and liver, and brought them along to the queen as a token.
sentences.i: And as a young wild boar came bounding along just then, he stabbed it, took out its lungs and liver, and brought them to the queen as proof.
sentences.n: Frischling = a young wild boar. “stach … ab”: abstechen (to stab, slaughter) is separable, as is “brachte … mit” (mitbringen, to bring along).

sentences.t: Der Koch mußte sie in Salz kochen, und das boshafte Weib aß sie auf und meinte sie hätte Sneewittchens Lunge und Leber gegessen.
sentences.l: The cook had to boil them in salt, and the wicked woman ate them up and supposed she had eaten Sneewittchen’s lung and liver.
sentences.i: The cook had to boil them in salt, and the wicked woman ate them up, believing she had eaten Snow-White’s lungs and liver.
sentences.n: mußte (1857 for musste) = had to. boshafte = spiteful, wicked. Weib is a plain (here contemptuous) word for “woman.” “aß … auf”: aufessen (to eat up) is separable.

sentences.t: Nun war das arme Kind in dem großen Wald mutterseelig allein, und ward ihm so angst, daß es alle Blätter an den Bäumen ansah und nicht wußte wie es sich helfen sollte.
sentences.l: Now the poor child was in the great forest all alone, forsaken by all, and it grew so frightened that it looked at all the leaves on the trees and did not know how it should help itself.
sentences.i: Now the poor child was all alone in the great forest, forsaken by every soul, and grew so frightened that she stared at every leaf on the trees and did not know what to do.
sentences.n: mutterseelig allein (standard mutterseelenallein) = utterly, forlornly alone. “ward ihm so angst” — an impersonal construction with the dative (“it grew frightened to it”); German expresses fear this way.

sentences.t: Da fieng es an zu laufen und lief über die spitzen Steine und durch die Dornen, und die wilden Thiere sprangen an ihm vorbei, aber sie thaten ihm nichts.
sentences.l: Then it began to run and ran over the sharp stones and through the thorns, and the wild beasts sprang past it, but they did it no harm.
sentences.i: Then she set off running, over the sharp stones and through the thorns, and the wild beasts sprang past her, but did her no harm.
sentences.n: “thaten ihm nichts” (thaten = 1857 taten, from thun/tun): “did it nothing,” i.e. did it no harm — a common idiom.

sentences.t: Es lief so lange nur die Füße noch fort konnten, bis es bald Abend werden wollte, da sah es ein kleines Häuschen und gieng hinein sich zu ruhen.
sentences.l: It ran only as long as its feet could still go on, until evening was almost coming, then it saw a little house and went inside to rest itself.
sentences.i: She ran on only as long as her feet would carry her, until evening was nearly falling, and then she saw a little house and went in to rest.
sentences.n: gieng (1857 for ging) = went. Häuschen is a diminutive of Haus. “sich zu ruhen” = to rest oneself.

sentences.t: In dem Häuschen war alles klein, aber so zierlich und reinlich, daß es nicht zu sagen ist.
sentences.l: In the little house everything was small, but so dainty and clean that it cannot be told.
sentences.i: Inside the little house everything was small, but so dainty and spotless that it cannot be described.
sentences.n: reinlich = clean, tidy. “daß es nicht zu sagen ist” = so that it cannot be told — a set way of saying “beyond description.”

sentences.t: Da stand ein weiß gedecktes Tischlein mit sieben kleinen Tellern, jedes Tellerlein mit seinem Löffelein, ferner sieben Messerlein und Gäblein, und sieben Becherlein.
sentences.l: There stood a white-laid little table with seven small plates, each little-plate with its little-spoon, further seven little-knives and little-forks, and seven little-cups.
sentences.i: There stood a little table laid with white, with seven small plates — each little plate with its little spoon — and, besides, seven little knives and forks and seven little cups.
sentences.n: The scene overflows with -lein / -chen diminutives (Tischlein, Tellerlein, Löffelein, Messerlein, Gäblein, Becherlein) to convey the tininess of the dwarves’ house. ferner = further, besides.

sentences.t: An der Wand waren sieben Bettlein neben einander aufgestellt und schneeweiße Laken darüber gedeckt.
sentences.l: On the wall seven little beds stood set up next to one another and snow-white sheets covered over them.
sentences.i: Against the wall seven little beds stood side by side, with snow-white sheets spread over them.
sentences.n: “neben einander aufgestellt”: aufstellen (to set up) separable. Laken = sheets. schneeweiße = snow-white.

sentences.t: Sneewittchen, weil es so hungrig und durstig war, aß von jedem Tellerlein ein wenig Gemüs und Brot, und trank aus jedem Becherlein einen Tropfen Wein; denn es wollte nicht einem allein alles wegnehmen.
sentences.l: Sneewittchen, because it was so hungry and thirsty, ate a little vegetable and bread from each little-plate, and drank a drop of wine out of each little-cup; for it did not want to take away everything from one alone.
sentences.i: Snow-White, being so hungry and thirsty, ate a little of the vegetables and bread from each little plate, and drank a drop of wine from each little cup; for she would not take everything from any one alone.
sentences.n: Gemüs (1857 for Gemüse) = vegetables. “einem allein alles wegnehmen”: wegnehmen (to take away) is separable; einem = “from one” (dative). Her fairness — taking only a little from each — marks her gentleness.

sentences.t: Hernach, weil es so müde war, legte es sich in ein Bettchen, aber keins paßte; das eine war zu lang, das andere zu kurz, bis endlich das siebente recht war: und darin blieb es liegen, befahl sich Gott und schlief ein.
sentences.l: Afterward, because it was so tired, it laid itself into a little bed, but none fitted; the one was too long, the other too short, until at last the seventh was right: and therein it remained lying, commended itself to God and fell asleep.
sentences.i: Afterward, being so tired, she lay down in one of the little beds, but none fitted — one was too long, another too short — until at last the seventh was just right; and there she stayed lying, commended herself to God, and fell asleep.
sentences.n: paßte (1857 for passte) = fitted. “befahl sich Gott” = commended herself to God (befehlen here in its older sense of entrusting). “schlief ein”: einschlafen (to fall asleep) separable.

sentences.t: Als es ganz dunkel geworden war, kamen die Herren von dem Häuslein, das waren die sieben Zwerge, die in den Bergen nach Erz hackten und gruben.
sentences.l: When it had become quite dark, the masters of the little house came home, those were the seven dwarves, who hacked and dug for ore in the mountains.
sentences.i: When it had grown quite dark, the masters of the little house came home — these were the seven dwarves, who hacked and dug for ore in the mountains.
sentences.n: Erz = ore. hackten und gruben = hacked and dug (gruben is the past plural of graben). The dwarves are miners.

sentences.t: Sie zündeten ihre sieben Lichtlein an, und wie es nun hell im Häuslein ward, sahen sie daß jemand darin gewesen war, denn es stand nicht alles so in der Ordnung, wie sie es verlassen hatten.
sentences.l: They lit their seven little lights, and as it now grew bright in the little house, they saw that someone had been inside, for not everything stood so in order as they had left it.
sentences.i: They lit their seven little lamps, and as the little house now grew bright, they saw that someone had been there, for not everything stood in the order in which they had left it.
sentences.n: “zündeten … an”: anzünden (to light, kindle) is separable. Lichtlein = little lights/candles. hell = bright.

sentences.t: Der erste sprach „wer hat auf meinem Stühlchen gesessen?“
sentences.l: The first spoke “who has sat on my little chair?”
sentences.i: The first said: “Who has been sitting on my little chair?”
sentences.n: The seven dwarves each ask in turn — a folk-tale sequence built on diminutives (Stühlchen, Tellerchen, Brötchen …). gesessen = sat (past participle of sitzen).

sentences.t: Der zweite „wer hat von meinem Tellerchen gegessen?“
sentences.l: The second “who has eaten from my little plate?”

sentences.t: Der dritte „wer hat von meinem Brötchen genommen?“
sentences.l: The third “who has taken from my little bread-roll?”
sentences.n: Brötchen = a little bread-roll. genommen = taken (past participle of nehmen).

sentences.t: Der vierte „wer hat von meinem Gemüschen gegessen?“
sentences.l: The fourth “who has eaten from my little vegetables?”
sentences.n: Gemüschen is a playful diminutive of Gemüs (vegetables).

sentences.t: Der fünfte „wer hat mit meinem Gäbelchen gestochen?“
sentences.l: The fifth “who has pricked with my little fork?”
sentences.n: Gäbelchen = little fork. gestochen = pricked (past participle of stechen).

sentences.t: Der sechste „wer hat mit meinem Messerchen geschnitten?“
sentences.l: The sixth “who has cut with my little knife?”
sentences.n: Messerchen = little knife. geschnitten = cut (past participle of schneiden).

sentences.t: Der siebente „wer hat aus meinem Becherlein getrunken?“
sentences.l: The seventh “who has drunk out of my little cup?”

sentences.t: Dann sah sich der erste um und sah daß auf seinem Bett eine kleine Dälle war, da sprach er „wer hat in mein Bettchen getreten?“
sentences.l: Then the first looked about him and saw that on his bed there was a little dent, then he spoke “who has stepped into my little bed?”
sentences.i: Then the first looked about him and saw that there was a little dent in his bed, and he said: “Who has been treading in my little bed?”
sentences.n: “sah sich … um”: sich umsehen (to look around) reflexive + separable. Dälle = a dent, hollow (a dialect word). getreten = stepped, trodden (past participle of treten).

sentences.t: Die andern kamen gelaufen und riefen „in meinem hat auch jemand gelegen.“
sentences.l: The others came running and cried “in mine too someone has lain.”
sentences.i: The others came running and cried: “Someone has lain in mine, too.”
sentences.n: “kamen gelaufen” — “came running,” the past participle used with kommen to show manner of arrival. gelegen = lain (past participle of liegen).

sentences.t: Der siebente aber, als er in sein Bett sah, erblickte Sneewittchen, das lag darin und schlief.
sentences.l: The seventh, however, when he looked into his bed, caught sight of Sneewittchen, who lay therein and slept.
sentences.i: But the seventh, when he looked into his bed, caught sight of Snow-White, who lay there asleep.
sentences.n: erblickte = caught sight of. “das lag darin” — das (neuter, for das Kind) resumes Sneewittchen.

sentences.t: Nun rief er die andern, die kamen herbeigelaufen, und schrien vor Verwunderung, holten ihre sieben Lichtlein, und beleuchteten Sneewittchen.
sentences.l: Now he called the others, they came running up, and shrieked with astonishment, fetched their seven little lights, and shone them upon Sneewittchen.
sentences.i: Now he called the others; they came running up and shrieked with astonishment, fetched their seven little lamps, and shone them on Snow-White.
sentences.n: herbeigelaufen = come running up (past participle; separable herbeilaufen). schrien = shrieked (past plural of schreien). beleuchteten = shone light upon.

sentences.t: „Ei, du mein Gott! ei, du mein Gott!“ riefen sie, „was ist das Kind so schön!“
sentences.l: “Oh, my goodness! oh, my goodness!” they cried, “how beautiful the child is!”
sentences.i: “Oh, good heavens! Oh, good heavens!” they cried; “how beautiful the child is!”
sentences.n: “Ei, du mein Gott!” is an exclamation of amazement — “oh my goodness!” “was ist das Kind so schön!” uses was … so … as an exclamation, “how beautiful the child is!”

sentences.t: und hatten so große Freude, daß sie es nicht aufweckten, sondern im Bettlein fortschlafen ließen.
sentences.l: and had so great joy that they did not wake it up, but let it sleep on in the little bed.
sentences.i: and they were so delighted that they did not wake her, but let her sleep on in the little bed.
sentences.n: “aufweckten”: aufwecken (to wake up) separable. “fortschlafen ließen”: fortschlafen (to sleep on) separable; lassen = to let.

sentences.t: Der siebente Zwerg aber schlief bei seinen Gesellen, bei jedem eine Stunde, da war die Nacht herum.
sentences.l: The seventh dwarf, however, slept with his companions, an hour with each, then the night was through.
sentences.i: The seventh dwarf, however, slept with his companions, an hour with each, and so the night passed.
sentences.n: “da war die Nacht herum” = then the night was over/through — herum sein of time = to be up, be past.

sentences.t: Als es Morgen war, erwachte Sneewittchen, und wie es die sieben Zwerge sah, erschrack es.
sentences.l: When it was morning, Sneewittchen awoke, and as it saw the seven dwarves, it took fright.
sentences.i: When morning came, Snow-White awoke, and when she saw the seven dwarves she took fright.
sentences.n: erwachte = awoke. erschrack (1857 erschrak) = took fright.

sentences.t: Sie waren aber freundlich und fragten „wie heißt du?“
sentences.l: But they were friendly and asked “what are you called?”
sentences.i: But they were kindly and asked: “What is your name?”
sentences.n: “wie heißt du?” = literally “how are you called?” — the ordinary way to ask a name. heißt = 2nd person of heißen (to be called).

sentences.t: „Ich heiße Sneewittchen,“ antwortete es.
sentences.l: “I am called Sneewittchen,” it answered.
sentences.i: “My name is Snow-White,” she answered.
sentences.n: heiße = 1st person of heißen: “I am called.”

sentences.t: „Wie bist du in unser Haus gekommen?“ sprachen weiter die Zwerge.
sentences.l: “How did you come into our house?” spoke the dwarves further.
sentences.i: “How did you come into our house?” the dwarves went on to ask.
sentences.n: unser = our. “sprachen weiter” = spoke further, went on speaking.

sentences.t: Da erzählte es ihnen daß seine Stiefmutter es hätte wollen umbringen lassen, der Jäger hätte ihm aber das Leben geschenkt, und da wär es gelaufen den ganzen Tag, bis es endlich ihr Häuslein gefunden hätte.
sentences.l: Then it told them that its stepmother had wanted to have it done away with, but the huntsman had granted it its life, and then it had run the whole day, until at last it had found their little house.
sentences.i: Then she told them that her stepmother had wanted to have her killed, but the huntsman had spared her life, and that she had then run the whole day until at last she found their little house.
sentences.n: A reported-speech chain in the subjunctive (hätte … wollen, hätte … geschenkt, wär … gelaufen, gefunden hätte) — German marks reported speech with the subjunctive. “umbringen lassen” = to have (someone) killed. “das Leben schenken” = to spare a life.

sentences.t: Die Zwerge sprachen „willst du unsern Haushalt versehen, kochen, betten, waschen, nähen und stricken, und willst du alles ordentlich und reinlich halten, so kannst du bei uns bleiben, und es soll dir an nichts fehlen.“
sentences.l: The dwarves spoke “if you will look after our household — cook, make the beds, wash, sew and knit — and if you will keep everything orderly and clean, then you can stay with us, and you shall want for nothing.”
sentences.i: The dwarves said: “If you will keep house for us — cook, make the beds, wash, sew and knit — and keep everything neat and clean, then you may stay with us, and you shall want for nothing.”
sentences.n: “den Haushalt versehen” = to look after the household. The verbs (kochen, betten, waschen, nähen, stricken) list the housework. “es soll dir an nichts fehlen” = you shall lack nothing (fehlen with the dative + an).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte Sneewittchen, „von Herzen gern,“ und blieb bei ihnen.
sentences.l: “Yes,” said Sneewittchen, “with all my heart,” and stayed with them.
sentences.i: “Yes,” said Snow-White, “gladly, with all my heart,” and she stayed with them.
sentences.n: “von Herzen gern” = gladly, from the heart — a warm assent.

sentences.t: Es hielt ihnen das Haus in Ordnung: Morgens giengen sie in die Berge und suchten Erz und Gold, Abends kamen sie wieder, und da mußte ihr Essen bereit sein.
sentences.l: It kept the house in order for them: in the mornings they went into the mountains and sought ore and gold, in the evenings they came back, and then their meal had to be ready.
sentences.i: She kept house for them: in the mornings they went into the mountains and sought ore and gold, and in the evenings they came back, and then their meal had to be ready.
sentences.n: giengen (1857 for gingen) = went. Note the day-rhythm nouns Morgens / Abends (in the mornings / evenings) with adverbial -s.

sentences.t: Den Tag über war das Mädchen allein, da warnten es die guten Zwerglein und sprachen „hüte dich vor deiner Stiefmutter, die wird bald wissen daß du hier bist; laß ja niemand herein.“
sentences.l: During the day the girl was alone, then the good little dwarves warned it and spoke “guard yourself against your stepmother, she will soon know that you are here; let no one in at all.”
sentences.i: During the day the girl was alone, and the good little dwarves warned her, saying: “Beware of your stepmother — she will soon know you are here; let no one in.”
sentences.n: warnten = warned. “hüte dich vor …” = beware of, guard yourself against (reflexive imperative). “laß ja niemand herein” — ja here intensifies the command: “be sure to let no one in.”

sentences.t: Die Königin aber, nachdem sie Sneewittchens Lunge und Leber glaubte gegessen zu haben, dachte nicht anders als sie wäre wieder die erste und allerschönste, trat vor ihren Spiegel und sprach
sentences.l: The queen, however, after she believed she had eaten Sneewittchen’s lung and liver, thought nothing other than that she was again the first and very fairest, stepped before her mirror and spoke
sentences.i: The queen, however, believing she had eaten Snow-White’s lungs and liver, supposed that she was once more the first and fairest of all, and she stepped before her mirror and said:
sentences.n: “dachte nicht anders als” = thought nothing other than, was sure that. allerschönste = the very fairest (aller- intensifies the superlative).

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: Da antwortete der Spiegel
sentences.l: Then answered the mirror
sentences.i: Then the mirror answered:

sentences.t:
„Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste hier,
aber Sneewittchen über den Bergen
bei den sieben Zwergen
ist noch tausendmal schöner als ihr.“
sentences.l:
“Lady Queen, you are the fairest here,
but Sneewittchen over the mountains
with the seven dwarves
is still a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.i: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest here, / but Snow-White beyond the mountains, with the seven dwarves, is a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.n: The mirror’s new answer, now that Snow-White lives with the dwarves. “über den Bergen / bei den sieben Zwergen” rhymes Bergen / Zwergen. noch = still, yet.

sentences.t: Da erschrack sie, denn sie wußte, daß der Spiegel keine Unwahrheit sprach, und merkte daß der Jäger sie betrogen hatte, und Sneewittchen noch am Leben war.
sentences.l: Then she was startled, for she knew that the mirror spoke no untruth, and perceived that the huntsman had deceived her, and Sneewittchen was still alive.
sentences.i: Then she was aghast, for she knew the mirror spoke no untruth, and she saw that the huntsman had deceived her and that Snow-White was still alive.
sentences.n: Unwahrheit = untruth. merkte = noticed, perceived. betrogen = deceived (past participle of betrügen).

sentences.t: Und da sann und sann sie aufs neue, wie sie es umbringen wollte; denn so lange sie nicht die schönste war im ganzen Land, ließ ihr der Neid keine Ruhe.
sentences.l: And then she pondered and pondered anew how she would do away with it; for as long as she was not the fairest in all the land, envy let her have no rest.
sentences.i: And then she pondered and pondered anew how she might do away with her; for as long as she was not the fairest in all the land, envy gave her no peace.
sentences.n: “sann und sann” = pondered and pondered (past of sinnen). umbringen = to do away with, kill. “aufs neue” = anew, afresh.

sentences.t: Und als sie sich endlich etwas ausgedacht hatte, färbte sie sich das Gesicht, und kleidete sich wie eine alte Krämerin, und war ganz unkenntlich.
sentences.l: And when she had at last thought out something, she colored her face, and dressed herself like an old pedlar-woman, and was quite unrecognizable.
sentences.i: And when she had at last devised something, she painted her face and dressed herself like an old pedlar-woman, so that she was quite unrecognizable.
sentences.n: “etwas ausgedacht”: sich etwas ausdenken = to think something up (separable). färbte = colored, dyed. Krämerin = a woman pedlar/shopkeeper. unkenntlich = unrecognizable.

sentences.t: In dieser Gestalt gieng sie über die sieben Berge zu den sieben Zwergen, klopfte an die Thüre, und rief „schöne Waare feil! feil!“
sentences.l: In this guise she went over the seven mountains to the seven dwarves, knocked at the door, and cried “fine wares for sale! for sale!”
sentences.i: In this guise she crossed the seven mountains to the seven dwarves, knocked at the door, and cried: “Fine wares for sale! For sale!”
sentences.n: Gestalt = guise, form. Thüre (1857 for Türe) = door. Waare (1857 for Ware) = wares, goods. feil = for sale (an old word, now only in feilbieten).

sentences.t: Sneewittchen guckte zum Fenster heraus und rief „guten Tag, liebe Frau, was habt ihr zu verkaufen?“
sentences.l: Sneewittchen peeped out of the window and cried “good day, dear woman, what have you to sell?”
sentences.i: Snow-White peeped out of the window and called: “Good day, dear woman, what have you to sell?”
sentences.n: guckte = peeped. verkaufen = to sell.

sentences.t: „Gute Waare, schöne Waare,“ antwortete sie, „Schnürriemen von allen Farben,“ und holte einen hervor, der aus bunter Seide geflochten war.
sentences.l: “Good wares, fine wares,” she answered, “laces of all colors,” and drew one forth that was braided out of colored silk.
sentences.i: “Good wares, fine wares,” she answered, “stay-laces of every color,” and she drew one out, braided of colored silk.
sentences.n: Schnürriemen = a lace (for a bodice). Farben = colors. geflochten = braided (past participle of flechten). bunter = of many colors.

sentences.t: „Die ehrliche Frau kann ich herein lassen“ dachte Sneewittchen, riegelte die Thüre auf und kaufte sich den hübschen Schnürriemen.
sentences.l: “The honest woman I can let in” thought Sneewittchen, unbolted the door and bought herself the pretty lace.
sentences.i: “I can let this honest woman in,” thought Snow-White, and she unbolted the door and bought the pretty lace.
sentences.n: ehrliche = honest. “riegelte … auf”: aufriegeln (to unbolt) is separable. hübschen = pretty.

sentences.t: „Kind,“ sprach die Alte, „wie du aussiehst! komm, ich will dich einmal ordentlich schnüren.“
sentences.l: “Child,” spoke the old woman, “how you look! come, I will lace you up properly for once.”
sentences.i: “Child,” said the old woman, “what a sight you are! Come, let me lace you up properly for once.”
sentences.n: die Alte = the old one/woman. “wie du aussiehst!” = “the state of you!” (aussehen = to look, appear; separable). schnüren = to lace.

sentences.t: Sneewittchen hatte kein Arg, stellte sich vor sie, und ließ sich mit dem neuen Schnürriemen schnüren: aber die Alte schnürte geschwind und schnürte so fest, daß dem Sneewittchen der Athem vergieng, und es für todt hinfiel.
sentences.l: Sneewittchen had no suspicion, placed itself before her, and let itself be laced with the new lace: but the old woman laced quickly and laced so tight that Sneewittchen’s breath failed and it fell down as if dead.
sentences.i: Snow-White had no suspicion, stood before her, and let herself be laced with the new lace; but the old woman laced quickly, and laced so tight that Snow-White’s breath failed her, and she fell down as if dead.
sentences.n: “kein Arg haben” = to have no suspicion, sense no harm. Athem (1857 for Atem) = breath. “vergieng” (1857 for verging) = failed, gave out. “für todt hinfiel” (todt = 1857 tot) = fell down for dead.

sentences.t: „Nun bist du die schönste gewesen“ sprach sie, und eilte hinaus.
sentences.l: “Now you have been the fairest” spoke she, and hurried out.
sentences.i: “Now you have been the fairest,” she said, and hurried out.
sentences.n: “bist du … gewesen” = you have been — the queen gloats that Snow-White’s beauty is now finished.

sentences.t: Nicht lange darauf, zur Abendzeit, kamen die sieben Zwerge nach Haus, aber wie erschraken sie, als sie ihr liebes Sneewittchen auf der Erde liegen sahen; und es regte und bewegte sich nicht, als wäre es todt.
sentences.l: Not long thereafter, at eventide, the seven dwarves came home, but how startled they were when they saw their dear Sneewittchen lying on the ground; and it stirred and moved not, as if it were dead.
sentences.i: Not long afterward, toward evening, the seven dwarves came home, and how startled they were to see their dear Snow-White lying on the ground; and she neither stirred nor moved, as if she were dead.
sentences.n: Abendzeit = evening time, eventide. erschraken = were startled (past plural). “regte und bewegte sich nicht” = stirred and moved not (both reflexive).

sentences.t: Sie hoben es in die Höhe, und weil sie sahen daß es zu fest geschnürt war, schnitten sie den Schnürriemen entzwei: da fieng es an ein wenig zu athmen, und ward nach und nach wieder lebendig.
sentences.l: They lifted it up into the height, and because they saw that it was laced too tight, they cut the lace in two: then it began to breathe a little, and became by and by alive again.
sentences.i: They lifted her up, and because they saw she was laced too tight, they cut the lace in two; then she began to breathe a little, and little by little came back to life.
sentences.n: hoben = lifted (past plural of heben). geschnürt = laced (past participle). “schnitten … entzwei” = cut in two (entzwei = apart, asunder). athmen (1857 for atmen) = to breathe. “nach und nach” = by and by, gradually.

sentences.t: Als die Zwerge hörten was geschehen war, sprachen sie, „die alte Krämerfrau war niemand als die gottlose Königin: hüte dich und laß keinen Menschen herein, wenn wir nicht bei dir sind.“
sentences.l: When the dwarves heard what had happened, they spoke, “the old pedlar-woman was none other than the godless queen: guard yourself and let no person in when we are not with you.”
sentences.i: When the dwarves heard what had happened, they said: “The old pedlar-woman was none other than the godless queen. Beware, and let no one in while we are not with you.”
sentences.n: geschehen = happened (past participle of geschehen). Krämerfrau = pedlar-woman. gottlose = godless, wicked. “laß keinen Menschen herein” = let no person in.

sentences.t: Das böse Weib aber, als es nach Haus gekommen war, gieng vor den Spiegel und fragte
sentences.l: The evil woman, however, when it had come home, went before the mirror and asked
sentences.i: But the evil woman, when she had come home, went before the mirror and asked:
sentences.n: “das böse Weib” — the child and the queen are both grammatically neuter here (Weib is neuter), so es = “she.” böse = evil.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: Da antwortete er wie sonst
sentences.l: Then answered he as usual
sentences.i: Then he answered as before:
sentences.n: “wie sonst” = as usual, as otherwise.

sentences.t:
„Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste hier,
aber Sneewittchen über den Bergen
bei den sieben Zwergen
ist noch tausendmal schöner als ihr.“
sentences.l:
“Lady Queen, you are the fairest here,
but Sneewittchen over the mountains
with the seven dwarves
is still a thousand times fairer than you.”

sentences.t: Als sie das hörte, lief ihr alles Blut zum Herzen, so erschrack sie, denn sie sah wohl daß Sneewittchen wieder lebendig geworden war.
sentences.l: When she heard that, all her blood ran to her heart, so startled was she, for she saw well that Sneewittchen had become alive again.
sentences.i: When she heard that, all her blood ran to her heart, so aghast was she, for she saw plainly that Snow-White had come back to life.
sentences.n: “lief ihr alles Blut zum Herzen” — a vivid idiom for shock, the blood rushing to the heart. wohl here = plainly, well.

sentences.t: „Nun aber,“ sprach sie, „will ich etwas aussinnen, das dich zu Grunde richten soll,“ und mit Hexenkünsten, die sie verstand, machte sie einen giftigen Kamm.
sentences.l: “But now,” spoke she, “I will think out something that shall bring you to ruin,” and with witch-arts that she understood, she made a poisonous comb.
sentences.i: “But now,” she said, “I will devise something that shall be your undoing,” and with the witch-arts she knew, she made a poisoned comb.
sentences.n: aussinnen = to think up, devise. “zu Grunde richten” = to ruin, destroy utterly. Hexenkünsten = witch-arts (dat. pl. of Hexenkunst). verstand = understood, knew (here: had mastered). giftigen = poisonous.

sentences.t: Dann verkleidete sie sich und nahm die Gestalt eines andern alten Weibes an.
sentences.l: Then she disguised herself and took on the shape of another old woman.
sentences.i: Then she disguised herself and took on the shape of a different old woman.
sentences.n: “verkleidete sich” = disguised herself. “nahm … an”: annehmen (to take on, assume) is separable. Weibes = of a woman (genitive of Weib).

sentences.t: So gieng sie hin über die sieben Berge zu den sieben Zwergen, klopfte an die Thüre, und rief „gute Waare feil! feil!“
sentences.l: So she went off over the seven mountains to the seven dwarves, knocked at the door, and cried “good wares for sale! for sale!”
sentences.i: So she went off over the seven mountains to the seven dwarves, knocked at the door, and cried: “Good wares for sale! For sale!”
sentences.n: “gieng … hin”: hingehen (to go off, go there) is separable.

sentences.t: Sneewittchen schaute heraus und sprach „geht nur weiter, ich darf niemand hereinlassen.“
sentences.l: Sneewittchen looked out and spoke “go on your way, I may let no one in.”
sentences.i: Snow-White looked out and said: “Go on your way — I am not allowed to let anyone in.”
sentences.n: schaute heraus = looked out. “ich darf niemand hereinlassen” — dürfen = to be allowed to; hereinlassen (to let in) is separable.

sentences.t: „Das Ansehen wird dir doch erlaubt sein“ sprach die Alte, zog den giftigen Kamm heraus und hielt ihn in die Höhe.
sentences.l: “The looking will surely be allowed you though” spoke the old woman, drew the poisonous comb out and held it up into the height.
sentences.i: “Surely you may at least look,” said the old woman, and she drew out the poisoned comb and held it up.
sentences.n: das Ansehen = the looking, a viewing (verbal noun of ansehen). erlaubt = allowed. “zog … heraus”: herausziehen (to pull out) separable. “in die Höhe” = up, aloft.

sentences.t: Da gefiel er dem Kinde so gut, daß es sich bethören ließ und die Thüre öffnete.
sentences.l: Then it pleased the child so well that it let itself be beguiled and opened the door.
sentences.i: Then it pleased the child so much that she let herself be beguiled and opened the door.
sentences.n: “gefiel … dem Kinde”: gefallen + dative = to please (someone). bethören (1857 for betören) = to beguile, befool. öffnete = opened.

sentences.t: Als sie des Kaufs einig waren, sprach die Alte „nun will ich dich einmal ordentlich kämmen.“
sentences.l: When they were agreed on the purchase, the old woman spoke “now I will comb you properly for once.”
sentences.i: When they had agreed on the bargain, the old woman said: “Now I will comb your hair properly for once.”
sentences.n: “des Kaufs einig” = agreed on the purchase (Kauf = purchase, deal; einig = in agreement). kämmen = to comb.

sentences.t: Das arme Sneewittchen dachte an nichts, und ließ die Alte gewähren, aber kaum hatte sie den Kamm in die Haare gesteckt, als das Gift darin wirkte, und das Mädchen ohne Besinnung niederfiel.
sentences.l: The poor Sneewittchen thought of nothing, and let the old woman have her way, but scarcely had she stuck the comb into the hair, when the poison in it took effect, and the girl fell down without consciousness.
sentences.i: Poor Snow-White suspected nothing, and let the old woman have her way; but scarcely had she thrust the comb into her hair when the poison in it took effect, and the girl sank down senseless.
sentences.n: “ließ … gewähren” = let (her) have her way. gesteckt = stuck, thrust (past participle of stecken). Gift = poison. wirkte = took effect. “ohne Besinnung” = senseless, unconscious. “niederfiel”: niederfallen (to fall down) separable.

sentences.t: „Du Ausbund von Schönheit,“ sprach das boshafte Weib, „jetzt ists um dich geschehen,“ und gieng fort.
sentences.l: “You paragon of beauty,” spoke the wicked woman, “now it is all over with you,” and went away.
sentences.i: “You paragon of beauty,” said the wicked woman, “now you are done for,” and off she went.
sentences.n: Ausbund = paragon, epitome (“Ausbund von Schönheit” = the very model of beauty). “jetzt ists um dich geschehen” = now it is all over with you, you are done for.

sentences.t: Zum Glück aber war es bald Abend, wo die sieben Zwerglein nach Haus kamen.
sentences.l: But luckily it was soon evening, when the seven little dwarves came home.
sentences.i: But by good luck it was soon evening, when the seven little dwarves came home.
sentences.n: “zum Glück” = luckily, by good fortune. Zwerglein = little dwarves (diminutive).

sentences.t: Als sie Sneewittchen wie todt auf der Erde liegen sahen, hatten sie gleich die Stiefmutter in Verdacht, suchten nach, und fanden den giftigen Kamm, und kaum hatten sie ihn herausgezogen, so kam Sneewittchen wieder zu sich, und erzählte was vorgegangen war.
sentences.l: When they saw Sneewittchen lying like dead on the ground, they at once had the stepmother under suspicion, searched about, and found the poisonous comb, and scarcely had they pulled it out, when Sneewittchen came to itself again, and told what had happened.
sentences.i: When they saw Snow-White lying as if dead on the ground, they at once suspected the stepmother, searched about, and found the poisoned comb; and scarcely had they drawn it out when Snow-White came to herself again and told what had happened.
sentences.n: “in Verdacht haben” = to have under suspicion, to suspect. “suchten nach” = searched about (nachsuchen). herausgezogen = drawn out (past participle; separable). “kam … zu sich” = came to herself. vorgegangen = happened, gone on (past participle of vorgehen).

sentences.t: Da warnten sie es noch einmal auf seiner Hut zu sein und niemand die Thüre zu öffnen.
sentences.l: Then they warned it once more to be on its guard and to open the door to no one.
sentences.i: Then they warned her once again to be on her guard and to open the door to no one.
sentences.n: “auf seiner Hut sein” = to be on one’s guard. öffnen = to open.

sentences.t: Die Königin stellte sich daheim vor den Spiegel und sprach
sentences.l: The queen placed herself at home before the mirror and spoke
sentences.i: At home the queen stood before the mirror and said:
sentences.n: “stellte sich … vor” = placed/stationed herself before. daheim = at home.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: Da antwortete er, wie vorher,
sentences.l: Then answered he, as before,
sentences.i: Then he answered, as before:

sentences.t:
„Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste hier,
aber Sneewittchen über den Bergen
bei den sieben Zwergen
ist doch noch tausendmal schöner als ihr.“
sentences.l:
“Lady Queen, you are the fairest here,
but Sneewittchen over the mountains
with the seven dwarves
is yet still a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.i: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest here, / but Snow-White beyond the mountains, with the seven dwarves, is still a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.n: The same answer, now reinforced with doch (“yet, all the same”) — the mirror will not relent.

sentences.t: Als sie den Spiegel so reden hörte, zitterte und bebte sie vor Zorn.
sentences.l: When she heard the mirror speak so, she trembled and quaked with wrath.
sentences.i: When she heard the mirror speak so, she trembled and quaked with rage.
sentences.n: zitterte und bebte = trembled and quaked. Zorn = wrath, rage.

sentences.t: „Sneewittchen soll sterben,“ rief sie, „und wenn es mein eignes Leben kostet.“
sentences.l: “Sneewittchen shall die,” cried she, “and if it costs my own life.”
sentences.i: “Snow-White shall die,” she cried, “even if it costs my own life.”
sentences.n: eignes (= eigenes) = own. kostet = costs. “und wenn” here = “even if.”

sentences.t: Darauf gieng sie in eine ganz verborgene einsame Kammer, wo niemand hinkam, und machte da einen giftigen giftigen Apfel.
sentences.l: Thereupon she went into a quite hidden, solitary chamber, where no one came, and made there a poisonous poisonous apple.
sentences.i: Thereupon she went into a quite hidden, lonely chamber, where no one ever came, and there she made a poisoned apple.
sentences.n: verborgene = hidden, secret. einsame = lonely, solitary. hinkam = came there (hinkommen, separable). The doubled “giftigen giftigen” stands in the 1857 text exactly as printed — kept as is.

sentences.t: Äußerlich sah er schön aus, weiß mit rothen Backen, daß jeder, der ihn erblickte, Lust danach bekam, aber wer ein Stückchen davon aß, der mußte sterben.
sentences.l: Outwardly it looked beautiful, white with red cheeks, so that everyone who caught sight of it got a longing for it, but whoever ate a little piece of it, that one had to die.
sentences.i: Outwardly it looked beautiful, white with red cheeks, so that anyone who saw it longed for it; but whoever ate a little piece of it was bound to die.
sentences.n: Äußerlich = outwardly. Backen = cheeks (here of the apple). “Lust danach bekam” = got a longing for it. “der mußte sterben” — the fronted der resumes “whoever.”

sentences.t: Als der Apfel fertig war, färbte sie sich das Gesicht, und verkleidete sich in eine Bauersfrau, und so gieng sie über die sieben Berge zu den sieben Zwergen.
sentences.l: When the apple was finished, she colored her face, and disguised herself as a peasant woman, and so she went over the seven mountains to the seven dwarves.
sentences.i: When the apple was ready, she painted her face and disguised herself as a peasant woman, and so she crossed the seven mountains to the seven dwarves.
sentences.n: fertig = ready, finished. “verkleidete sich in eine Bauersfrau” = disguised herself as a peasant woman.

sentences.t: Sie klopfte an, Sneewittchen streckte den Kopf zum Fenster heraus, und sprach „ich darf keinen Menschen einlassen, die sieben Zwerge haben mirs verboten.“
sentences.l: She knocked, Sneewittchen stretched its head out of the window, and spoke “I may let no person in, the seven dwarves have forbidden me it.”
sentences.i: She knocked; Snow-White stretched her head out of the window and said: “I may let no one in — the seven dwarves have forbidden me.”
sentences.n: “streckte den Kopf … heraus” = stretched her head out. einlassen = to let in. “haben mirs verboten” = have forbidden me it (mirs = mir es).

sentences.t: „Mir auch recht,“ antwortete die Bäurin, „meine Äpfel will ich schon los werden. Da, einen will ich dir schenken.“
sentences.l: “All right with me,” answered the peasant woman, “my apples I will surely get rid of. Here, one I will give you.”
sentences.i: “That’s fine by me,” answered the peasant woman; “I’ll be rid of my apples soon enough. Here, let me give you one.”
sentences.n: “mir auch recht” = fine by me. Bäurin = peasant woman. “los werden” = to get rid of. schenken = to give (as a gift).

sentences.t: „Nein,“ sprach Sneewittchen, „ich darf nichts annehmen.“
sentences.l: “No,” spoke Sneewittchen, “I may take nothing.”
sentences.i: “No,” said Snow-White, “I am not allowed to take anything.”
sentences.n: annehmen = to accept, take (separable).

sentences.t: „Fürchtest du dich vor Gift?“ sprach die Alte, „siehst du, da schneide ich den Apfel in zwei Theile; den rothen Backen iß du, den weißen will ich essen.“
sentences.l: “Are you afraid of poison?” spoke the old woman, “see, here I cut the apple into two parts; the red cheek eat you, the white will I eat.”
sentences.i: “Are you afraid of poison?” said the old woman. “Look, I’ll cut the apple in two: you eat the red cheek, and I will eat the white.”
sentences.n: “fürchtest du dich vor …” = are you afraid of (reflexive). schneide = cut (1st sg. of schneiden). Theile (1857 for Teile) = parts. iß (1857 for iss) = eat (imperative). The trick turns on the halves.

sentences.t: Der Apfel war aber so künstlich gemacht, daß der rothe Backen allein vergiftet war.
sentences.l: The apple, however, was so artfully made that the red cheek alone was poisoned.
sentences.i: But the apple was so cunningly made that the red cheek alone was poisoned.
sentences.n: künstlich = artful, ingeniously made. vergiftet = poisoned.

sentences.t: Sneewittchen lusterte den schönen Apfel an, und als es sah, daß die Bäurin davon aß, so konnte es nicht länger widerstehen, streckte die Hand hinaus und nahm die giftige Hälfte.
sentences.l: Sneewittchen craved the beautiful apple, and when it saw that the peasant woman ate of it, it could no longer resist, stretched out the hand and took the poisonous half.
sentences.i: Snow-White longed for the beautiful apple, and when she saw the peasant woman eat of it, she could resist no longer, reached out her hand, and took the poisoned half.
sentences.n: “lusterte … an” = eyed with longing, craved (anlüstern, a dialectal separable verb). widerstehen = to resist. Hälfte = half.

sentences.t: Kaum aber hatte es einen Bissen davon im Mund, so fiel es todt zur Erde nieder.
sentences.l: But scarcely had it a bite of it in the mouth, when it fell down dead to the ground.
sentences.i: But scarcely had she a bite of it in her mouth when she fell down dead to the ground.
sentences.n: Bissen = bite, mouthful. “fiel … todt zur Erde nieder”: niederfallen (to fall down) separable; todt (1857) = dead.

sentences.t: Da betrachtete es die Königin mit grausigen Blicken und lachte überlaut, und sprach „weiß wie Schnee, roth wie Blut, schwarz wie Ebenholz! diesmal können dich die Zwerge nicht wieder erwecken.“
sentences.l: Then the queen regarded it with gruesome looks and laughed out loud, and spoke “white as snow, red as blood, black as ebony! this time the dwarves cannot wake you again.”
sentences.i: Then the queen looked at her with dreadful glee and laughed aloud, and said: “White as snow, red as blood, black as ebony! This time the dwarves cannot wake you again.”
sentences.n: betrachtete = regarded, looked at. grausigen = gruesome, dreadful. überlaut = very loud, out loud. diesmal = this time. erwecken = to awaken, revive. The three colors return as the queen’s cruel triumph.

sentences.t: Und als sie daheim den Spiegel befragte,
sentences.l: And when at home she questioned the mirror,
sentences.i: And when, back at home, she questioned the mirror:
sentences.n: befragte = questioned, consulted. daheim = at home.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: so antwortete er endlich
sentences.l: so answered he at last
sentences.i: he answered at last:

sentences.t: „Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste im Land.“
sentences.l: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest in the land.”

sentences.t: Da hatte ihr neidisches Herz Ruhe, so gut ein neidisches Herz Ruhe haben kann.
sentences.l: Then her envious heart had rest, so far as an envious heart can have rest.
sentences.i: Then her envious heart had peace — so far as an envious heart can have peace.
sentences.n: neidisches = envious. A dry aside from the narrator: an envious heart can never truly be at peace.

sentences.t: Die Zwerglein, wie sie Abends nach Haus kamen, fanden Sneewittchen auf der Erde liegen, und es gieng kein Athem mehr aus seinem Mund, und es war todt.
sentences.l: The little dwarves, when they came home in the evening, found Sneewittchen lying on the ground, and no breath came anymore out of its mouth, and it was dead.
sentences.i: The little dwarves, when they came home in the evening, found Snow-White lying on the ground, and no breath came from her mouth any more, and she was dead.
sentences.n: Athem (1857 for Atem) = breath. todt (1857 for tot) = dead.

sentences.t: Sie hoben es auf, suchten ob sie was giftiges fänden, schnürten es auf, kämmten ihm die Haare, wuschen es mit Wasser und Wein, aber es half alles nichts; das liebe Kind war todt und blieb todt.
sentences.l: They lifted it up, searched whether they might find anything poisonous, unlaced it, combed its hair, washed it with water and wine, but it all helped nothing; the dear child was dead and remained dead.
sentences.i: They lifted her up, searched to see whether they could find anything poisonous, unlaced her, combed her hair, washed her with water and wine, but nothing was of any use; the dear child was dead and stayed dead.
sentences.n: “hoben … auf”: aufheben (to lift up) separable. fänden = might find (past subjunctive of finden). “schnürten … auf”: aufschnüren (to unlace) separable. kämmten = combed. wuschen = washed (past plural of waschen).

sentences.t: Sie legten es auf eine Bahre und setzten sich alle siebene daran und beweinten es, und weinten drei Tage lang.
sentences.l: They laid it on a bier and sat down all seven around it and wept over it, and wept three days long.
sentences.i: They laid her on a bier and all seven sat down around it and mourned her, and wept for three whole days.
sentences.n: Bahre = bier. “alle siebene” = all seven. beweinten = wept over, mourned. weinten = wept.

sentences.t: Da wollten sie es begraben, aber es sah noch so frisch aus wie ein lebender Mensch, und hatte noch seine schönen rothen Backen.
sentences.l: Then they wanted to bury it, but it still looked so fresh as a living person, and still had its beautiful red cheeks.
sentences.i: Then they wished to bury her, but she still looked as fresh as a living person, and still had her lovely red cheeks.
sentences.n: begraben = to bury. lebender = living (present participle of leben). rothen (1857 for roten) = red.

sentences.t: Sie sprachen „das können wir nicht in die schwarze Erde versenken,“ und ließen einen durchsichtigen Sarg von Glas machen, daß man es von allen Seiten sehen konnte, legten es hinein, und schrieben mit goldenen Buchstaben seinen Namen darauf, und daß es eine Königstochter wäre.
sentences.l: They spoke “this we cannot sink into the black earth,” and had a transparent coffin of glass made, so that one could see it from all sides, laid it inside, and wrote its name on it with golden letters, and that it was a king’s daughter.
sentences.i: They said: “We cannot lower this into the black earth,” and they had a transparent coffin of glass made, so that she could be seen from every side; they laid her inside, and wrote her name on it in golden letters, and that she was a king’s daughter.
sentences.n: versenken = to sink, lower (into). durchsichtigen = transparent. Sarg = coffin. geschrieben = written. Königstochter = a king’s daughter, princess.

sentences.t: Dann setzten sie den Sarg hinaus auf den Berg, und einer von ihnen blieb immer dabei, und bewachte ihn.
sentences.l: Then they set the coffin out on the mountain, and one of them always stayed by it, and watched over it.
sentences.i: Then they set the coffin out on the mountain, and one of them always stayed beside it and kept watch over it.
sentences.n: “setzten … hinaus” = set out. bewachte = guarded, watched over.

sentences.t: Und die Thiere kamen auch und beweinten Sneewittchen, erst eine Eule, dann ein Rabe, zuletzt ein Täubchen.
sentences.l: And the animals came too and wept over Sneewittchen, first an owl, then a raven, at last a little dove.
sentences.i: And the animals came too and mourned Snow-White — first an owl, then a raven, and at last a little dove.
sentences.n: Eule = owl. Rabe = raven. zuletzt = at last. Täubchen = a little dove (diminutive of Taube).

sentences.t: Nun lag Sneewittchen lange lange Zeit in dem Sarg und verweste nicht, sondern sah aus als wenn es schliefe, denn es war noch so weiß als Schnee, so roth als Blut, und so schwarzhaarig wie Ebenholz.
sentences.l: Now Sneewittchen lay a long, long time in the coffin and did not decay, but looked as if it were sleeping, for it was still as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black-haired as ebony.
sentences.i: Now Snow-White lay a long, long time in the coffin and did not decay, but looked as if she were only sleeping, for she was still as white as snow, as red as blood, and as black-haired as ebony.
sentences.n: verweste = decayed, rotted (past of verwesen). schliefe = were sleeping (past subjunctive of schlafen). The three colors, undimmed, mark her preservation.

sentences.t: Es geschah aber, daß ein Königssohn in den Wald gerieth und zu dem Zwergenhaus kam, da zu übernachten.
sentences.l: It happened, however, that a king’s son got into the forest and came to the dwarf-house, to pass the night there.
sentences.i: It happened, however, that a king’s son came into the forest and reached the dwarves’ house, to spend the night there.
sentences.n: geschah = happened. Königssohn = a king’s son, prince. Zwergenhaus = the dwarves’ house. übernachten = to pass the night.

sentences.t: Er sah auf dem Berg den Sarg, und das schöne Sneewittchen darin, und las was mit goldenen Buchstaben darauf geschrieben war.
sentences.l: He saw on the mountain the coffin, and the beautiful Sneewittchen in it, and read what was written on it with golden letters.
sentences.i: He saw the coffin on the mountain, and the beautiful Snow-White within it, and read what was written on it in golden letters.
sentences.n: geschrieben = written. The golden inscription is what draws him in.

sentences.t: Da sprach er zu den Zwergen „laßt mir den Sarg, ich will euch geben, was ihr dafür haben wollt.“
sentences.l: Then he spoke to the dwarves “leave me the coffin, I will give you what you want to have for it.”
sentences.i: Then he said to the dwarves: “Let me have the coffin; I will give you whatever you want for it.”
sentences.n: “laßt mir den Sarg” = leave/let me have the coffin. wollt = want (2nd pl. of wollen).

sentences.t: Aber die Zwerge antworteten „wir geben ihn nicht um alles Gold in der Welt.“
sentences.l: But the dwarves answered “we do not give it for all the gold in the world.”
sentences.i: But the dwarves answered: “We will not give it for all the gold in the world.”
sentences.n: “um alles Gold in der Welt” = for all the gold in the world — a set phrase of refusal.

sentences.t: Da sprach er „so schenkt mir ihn, denn ich kann nicht leben ohne Sneewittchen zu sehen, ich will es ehren und hochachten wie mein Liebstes.“
sentences.l: Then he spoke “so give it to me as a gift, for I cannot live without seeing Sneewittchen, I will honor and esteem it like my dearest.”
sentences.i: Then he said: “Then make me a gift of it, for I cannot live without seeing Snow-White; I will honor and cherish her as my dearest treasure.”
sentences.n: schenkt = give as a gift (imperative of schenken). ehren = to honor. hochachten = to esteem highly (separable). “mein Liebstes” = my dearest.

sentences.t: Wie er so sprach, empfanden die guten Zwerglein Mitleiden mit ihm und gaben ihm den Sarg.
sentences.l: As he spoke so, the good little dwarves felt pity with him and gave him the coffin.
sentences.i: As he spoke so, the good little dwarves felt pity for him and gave him the coffin.
sentences.n: empfanden = felt (past plural of empfinden). “Mitleiden mit ihm” = pity for him.

sentences.t: Der Königssohn ließ ihn nun von seinen Dienern auf den Schultern forttragen.
sentences.l: The king’s son now had it carried away by his servants on their shoulders.
sentences.i: The prince now had it carried off on the shoulders of his servants.
sentences.n: “ließ … forttragen” = had (it) carried off (fort-tragen, separable). Dienern = servants (dat. pl. of Diener).

sentences.t: Da geschah es, daß sie über einen Strauch stolperten, und von dem Schüttern fuhr der giftige Apfelgrütz, den Sneewittchen abgebissen hatte, aus dem Hals.
sentences.l: Then it happened that they stumbled over a bush, and from the jolting the poisonous piece of apple, which Sneewittchen had bitten off, flew out of the throat.
sentences.i: Then it happened that they stumbled over a bush, and from the jolt the poisonous bit of apple that Snow-White had bitten off flew out of her throat.
sentences.n: stolperten = stumbled. Strauch = bush. Schüttern = jolting, shaking. Apfelgrütz = a bit of apple-mush (a rare word: Grütz = groats/mush). abgebissen = bitten off (past participle of abbeißen, separable).

sentences.t: Und nicht lange so öffnete es die Augen, hob den Deckel vom Sarg in die Höhe, und richtete sich auf, und war wieder lebendig.
sentences.l: And not long, then it opened the eyes, lifted the lid off the coffin up into the height, and raised itself up, and was alive again.
sentences.i: And before long she opened her eyes, lifted the lid off the coffin, sat up, and was alive again.
sentences.n: “nicht lange so” = before long. Deckel = lid. “richtete sich auf” = raised herself up, sat up (sich aufrichten, separable). lebendig = alive.

sentences.t: „Ach Gott, wo bin ich?“ rief es.
sentences.l: “Ah God, where am I?” cried it.
sentences.i: “Oh God, where am I?” she cried.
sentences.n: The child’s first words on waking. rief = cried.

sentences.t: Der Königssohn sagte voll Freude „du bist bei mir,“ und erzählte was sich zugetragen hatte und sprach „ich habe dich lieber als alles auf der Welt; komm mit mir in meines Vaters Schloß, du sollst meine Gemahlin werden.“
sentences.l: The king’s son said, full of joy, “you are with me,” and told what had come to pass and spoke “I have you dearer than everything in the world; come with me into my father’s castle, you shall become my wife.”
sentences.i: The prince said, full of joy: “You are with me,” and he told her what had come to pass, and said: “I love you more than anything in the world; come with me to my father’s castle — you shall be my wife.”
sentences.n: zugetragen = come to pass (past participle of sich zutragen). “ich habe dich lieber als …” = I love you more than … Schloß (1857 for Schloss) = castle. “meines Vaters Schloß” = my father’s castle (genitive). Gemahlin = wife, consort.

sentences.t: Da war ihm Sneewittchen gut und gieng mit ihm, und ihre Hochzeit ward mit großer Pracht und Herrlichkeit angeordnet.
sentences.l: Then Sneewittchen was fond of him and went with him, and their wedding was arranged with great pomp and splendor.
sentences.i: Then Snow-White grew fond of him and went with him, and their wedding was arranged with great pomp and splendor.
sentences.n: “war ihm … gut” = was well-disposed to him, fond of him. Herrlichkeit = splendor, magnificence. angeordnet = arranged (past participle of anordnen, separable).

sentences.t: Zu dem Fest wurde aber auch Sneewittchens gottlose Stiefmutter eingeladen.
sentences.l: To the feast, however, was also invited Sneewittchen’s godless stepmother.
sentences.i: To the feast, however, Snow-White’s godless stepmother too was invited.
sentences.n: Fest = feast, celebration. gottlose = godless. eingeladen = invited (past participle of einladen, separable).

sentences.t: Wie sie sich nun mit schönen Kleidern angethan hatte, trat sie vor den Spiegel und sprach
sentences.l: When she had now arrayed herself in beautiful clothes, she stepped before the mirror and spoke
sentences.i: When she had arrayed herself in fine clothes, she stepped before the mirror and said:
sentences.n: “angethan hatte” — sich anthun (1857 for antun) here = to array/dress oneself (separable). angethan = put on, donned.

sentences.t:
„Spieglein, Spieglein an der Wand,
wer ist die schönste im ganzen Land?“
sentences.l:
“Little-mirror, little-mirror on the wall,
who is the fairest in all the land?”

sentences.t: Der Spiegel antwortete
sentences.l: The mirror answered
sentences.i: The mirror answered:

sentences.t:
„Frau Königin, ihr seid die schönste hier,
aber die junge Königin ist tausendmal schöner als ihr.“
sentences.l:
“Lady Queen, you are the fairest here,
but the young queen is a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.i: “Lady Queen, you are the fairest here, / but the young queen is a thousand times fairer than you.”
sentences.n: The last answer: “die junge Königin” is Snow-White, now a queen herself. The rhyme returns to hier / ihr.

sentences.t: Da stieß das böse Weib einen Fluch aus, und ward ihr so angst, so angst, daß sie sich nicht zu lassen wußte.
sentences.l: Then the evil woman uttered a curse, and grew so frightened, so frightened, that she did not know what to do with herself.
sentences.i: Then the evil woman let out a curse, and grew so afraid, so afraid, that she did not know what to do with herself.
sentences.n: “stieß … einen Fluch aus”: ausstoßen (to utter, let out) separable. Fluch = curse. “sich nicht zu lassen wissen” = not to know what to do with oneself (from distress).

sentences.t: Sie wollte zuerst gar nicht auf die Hochzeit kommen: doch ließ es ihr keine Ruhe, sie mußte fort und die junge Königin sehen.
sentences.l: She wanted at first not to come to the wedding at all: yet it left her no rest, she had to go and see the young queen.
sentences.i: At first she did not want to come to the wedding at all; yet it gave her no peace — she had to go and see the young queen.
sentences.n: zuerst = at first. “ließ es ihr keine Ruhe” = it left her no rest. “sie mußte fort” = she had to be off.

sentences.t: Und wie sie hineintrat, erkannte sie Sneewittchen, und vor Angst und Schrecken stand sie da und konnte sich nicht regen.
sentences.l: And as she stepped in, she recognized Sneewittchen, and for fear and terror she stood there and could not stir herself.
sentences.i: And as she entered, she recognized Snow-White, and for fear and terror she stood rooted there and could not move.
sentences.n: hineintrat = stepped in (hineintreten, separable). erkannte = recognized. Schrecken = terror. “sich regen” = to stir, move.

sentences.t: Aber es waren schon eiserne Pantoffeln über Kohlenfeuer gestellt und wurden mit Zangen herein getragen und vor sie hingestellt.
sentences.l: But there were already iron slippers set over a coal fire, and they were carried in with tongs and set down before her.
sentences.i: But iron slippers had already been set over a coal fire, and they were carried in with tongs and set down before her.
sentences.n: Pantoffeln = slippers. “über Kohlenfeuer gestellt” = set over a coal fire. Zangen = tongs. “vor sie hingestellt” = set down before her. The tale’s grim ending is prepared.

sentences.t: Da mußte sie in die rothglühenden Schuhe treten und so lange tanzen, bis sie todt zur Erde fiel.
sentences.l: Then she had to step into the red-glowing shoes and dance so long until she fell down dead to the ground.
sentences.i: Then she had to step into the red-hot shoes and dance until she fell down dead upon the ground.
sentences.n: mußte = had to. rothglühenden (1857 spelling) = red-glowing, red-hot. Grimm does not soften the punishment: she is made to dance in the burning iron shoes until she dies.
